policy.title: RBI Finalizes Climate Scenario Analysis Requirements for Scheduled Commercial Banks
policy.description: RBI issued final guidelines requiring all scheduled commercial banks with assets over ₹1 lakh crore to conduct climate scenario analysis by March 2027, with smaller banks following by March 2028. Banks must integrate physical and transition risk metrics into ICAAP submissions starting FY27.

policy.title: SEBI Extends BRSR Core to Top 500 Listed Companies
policy.description: SEBI mandated BRSR Core reporting with reasonable assurance for the top 500 companies by market capitalization from FY27, expanding from the current top 150. The framework now includes Scope 3 emissions reporting for material categories.
policy.source: Securities and Exchange Board of India

policy.title: IRDAI Issues Draft Guidelines on Index-Based Parametric Insurance Products
policy.description: IRDAI released draft regulations establishing approval pathways for parametric insurance products tied to weather indices, requiring minimum 90% correlation between index triggers and actual losses. Public comments are due by 30 April 2026.
policy.source: Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority of India

policy.title: Basel Committee Publishes Pillar 3 Climate Risk Disclosure Template
policy.description: BCBS issued a standardized disclosure template for climate-related financial risks under Pillar 3, with implementation expected from January 2028. The template requires banks to disclose financed emissions, physical risk exposures by geography, and transition risk metrics by sector.

policy.title: ISSB Releases Guidance on Emerging Market Application of IFRS S2
policy.description: The ISSB published application guidance specifically addressing challenges for emerging market jurisdictions implementing IFRS S2, including proportionality provisions for smaller entities and sector-specific metrics for agriculture and informal economies.

policy.title: NGFS Updates Climate Scenarios with Enhanced Physical Risk Pathways
policy.description: NGFS released Version 5 of its climate scenarios incorporating updated IPCC AR6 physical risk projections and refined damage functions. The scenarios now include acute physical risk shocks as separate stress events alongside chronic pathways.
policy.source: Network for Greening the Financial System

competitors.title: Jupiter Intelligence Partners with HDFC Bank for India Physical Risk Assessment
competitors.description: Jupiter Intelligence announced a strategic partnership with HDFC Bank to provide physical climate risk analytics for the bank's commercial lending portfolio. The engagement covers flood, cyclone, and heat stress modeling across 15 Indian states.

competitors.title: Sprih Raises $12M Series A for ESG SaaS Platform Expansion
competitors.description: Bengaluru-based Sprih closed a $12 million Series A led by Accel India to expand its ESG management platform targeting mid-market Indian enterprises. The company plans to add BRSR Core automation and Scope 3 calculation modules.

competitors.title: Moody's Launches Physical Risk Scoring for Indian Corporate Bonds
competitors.description: Moody's ESG Solutions introduced physical climate risk scores for over 800 Indian corporate bond issuers, incorporating flood, drought, and cyclone exposure at asset level. The scores are integrated into Moody's CreditView platform.

competitors.title: XDI Releases APAC Banks Physical Risk Benchmark Report
competitors.description: XDI published its inaugural physical risk benchmark covering 50 major APAC banks' disclosed asset exposures, ranking institutions by flood and cyclone vulnerability. Three Indian public sector banks ranked in the highest risk decile.

competitors.title: Watershed Partners with Tata Group for Enterprise Carbon Management
competitors.description: Watershed announced an enterprise-wide partnership with Tata Group to deploy its carbon accounting platform across 12 Tata companies. The deal represents Watershed's largest emerging market engagement to date.

competitors.title: CarbonMint Signs MoU with NABARD for Agricultural Carbon Credit Verification
competitors.description: CarbonMint entered a partnership with NABARD to develop blockchain-based verification systems for smallholder agricultural carbon credits. The pilot covers 50,000 farmers across Maharashtra and Karnataka.

research.title: Climate Risk and Bank Lending: Evidence from Emerging Markets
research.description: Analysis of 2,400 banks across 45 emerging markets finds a 12-18% increase in NPAs for loan portfolios with high physical climate exposure post-extreme weather events. Indian banks showed above-average vulnerability due to agricultural lending concentration.

research.title: Parametric Insurance for Climate Resilience in South Asia
research.description: Swiss Re estimates the addressable market for parametric climate insurance in South Asia at $4.2 billion annually by 2030, with India comprising 65% of potential premiums. Flood and drought products show highest demand from agricultural and SME segments.

research.title: Integrating Physical Climate Risk into Credit Risk Frameworks
research.description: BIS working paper proposes methodologies for translating physical hazard data into PD and LGD adjustments, finding that current bank models underestimate climate-related credit losses by 25-40%. The paper recommends asset-level geospatial risk scoring.

research.title: ESG Data Gaps in Emerging Markets: Challenges and Solutions
research.description: IFC survey of 180 institutional investors reveals 73% cite data availability as the primary barrier to increasing emerging market ESG allocations. The report identifies India, Brazil, and Indonesia as priority markets for improved corporate sustainability data.

research.title: Climate Stress Testing: Lessons from Early Adopters
research.description: UNEPFI analysis of climate stress tests conducted by 35 banks globally finds significant variation in methodologies and results. The report recommends standardization of hazard data inputs and calls for regulator-specified scenario parameters.

research.title: Physical Climate Risk Assessment of Indian Banking Sector
research.description: CRISIL estimates ₹8.5 lakh crore of Indian bank lending is exposed to high physical climate risk, concentrated in agriculture (42%), real estate (28%), and infrastructure (18%). Coastal districts in Gujarat, Maharashtra, and Tamil Nadu show highest exposure density.

ourRead.headline: RBI's March 2027 deadline creates an 11-month window where StepChange's climate risk models become table stakes for Indian banks—but global players are already circling.
body: This week's signals converge on a single theme: the institutionalization of climate risk in Indian banking is accelerating faster than expected, with regulatory mandates hardening and global competitors smelling opportunity. The RBI's finalized scenario analysis requirements, SEBI's BRSR Core expansion, and IRDAI's parametric insurance draft all dropped within 45 days—a coordinated policy push that validates StepChange's strategic bets.

The RBI's March 2027 deadline for climate scenario analysis (policy-1) is the most consequential development. With 18 months until compliance, every scheduled commercial bank above ₹1 lakh crore is now a forced buyer of physical and transition risk analytics. The BIS Pillar 3 template (policy-4) adds international pressure for standardized disclosure. For StepChange, this means our PD/LGD models must be regulator-ready—aligned to NGFS scenarios and capable of integration into ICAAP submissions. The CRISIL research (research-6) quantifying ₹8.5 lakh crore in high-exposure lending provides the baseline threat assessment we should be citing in every bank pitch.

Jupiter Intelligence's HDFC partnership (comp-1) is the competitive signal that should concern us most. A global player has landed a flagship Indian private bank, demonstrating that sophisticated FIs will pay for best-in-class foreign solutions rather than wait for local alternatives. Meanwhile, Moody's physical risk scores for Indian bonds (comp-3) and XDI's APAC benchmark (comp-4) show that global data providers are building India-specific products. Sprih's $12M raise (comp-2) indicates the broader India ESG space is attracting growth capital. StepChange's moat must be depth of local hazard data and regulatory alignment—not just presence in India.

The BIS working paper on PD/LGD integration (research-3) is a gift for product development: it validates our core thesis that banks underestimate climate credit losses by 25-40% and explicitly recommends asset-level geospatial scoring—our exact methodology. Swiss Re's $4.2 billion parametric market sizing for South Asia (research-2) should accelerate our parametric insurance conversations with banks and insurers. The IFC data gaps report (research-4) confirms global investor demand for the emerging market ESG data that StepChange can uniquely provide.

Watch: IRDAI's parametric insurance consultation closes April 30—we should submit detailed comments positioning StepChange as a technical partner for index design. Action: Fast-track a pilot proposal to at least two public sector banks named in XDI's high-risk benchmark—they face regulatory and reputational pressure and need solutions before Jupiter locks up the private bank market.
